full_response:
Let the Kruskal–Szekeres coordinates (U , V) be defined from the Schwarzschild null coordinates  
u = t – r∗ , v = t + r∗  (r∗ = r + 2GM ln|r/2GM – 1|)

by  

U = −e^{−u/4GM},  V =  e^{ v/4GM}.                                 (1)

With this definition the metric becomes  

ds² = −\frac{32 G^{3}M^{3}}{r}\;e^{-\,r/2GM}\,dU\,dV + r² dΩ² ,     (2)

where r(U , V) is obtained implicitly from the product U V :

U V = −e^{−u/4GM}\,e^{v/4GM}
    = −e^{(v−u)/4GM}
    = −e^{2 r∗/4GM}
    = −e^{r∗/2GM}.                                            (3)

Insert r∗ = r + 2GM ln|r/2GM − 1| :

UV = −e^{r/2GM}\Bigl(\frac{r}{2GM} − 1\Bigr)
    = \bigl(1 − \frac{r}{2GM}\bigr)\,e^{r/2GM} ≡ F(r).         (4)

Behaviour of F(r)

• r → 0⁺  : F(r) → 1  
• r = 2GM : F(r) = 0  
• r → ∞  : F(r) → −∞  

and dF/dr < 0 for all r>0, so F(r) is strictly decreasing.  
Therefore the map r ↦ F(r) is one-to-one from (0,∞) onto (−∞,1).

Hence
 r(U , V) > 0 ⇔ F(r) = U V < 1.                               (5)

There are no separate restrictions on the individual signs of U and V; all four quadrants of the (U , V) plane contribute, provided the product satisfies (5).

\boxed{\;U V < 1\;}    (the entire Kruskal plane below the hyperbola U V = 1)

At the boundary UV = 1 the Schwarzschild radius shrinks to r = 0, the physical singularity where the coordinates cease to be regular.

Confidence: 74%
